content.full_text:
Staie of California 
To 
From 
ora 
Opinion Request Meeting Participants 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Ted Prim 
Date 
6-6-77 
Subject; 
Disclosure of income of trusts. 
At the opinion request meeting of May 20, 1977, the staff 
determined that the income from the assets of a trust should 
be reported in the same way as the income from the clients 
or assets of a business entity. 
Thus, Section 87207(b) is 
the applicable provision. 
If the trust owns a business 
entity, it probably is a business trust and therefore a 
business entity pursuant to Section 82004. 
If an entity 
owned by a trust performs brokerage services, income from 
that asset should be reported pursuant to 87207(b) (2). 
If, 
however, the trust owns an asset which performs services 
other than legal or brokerage services, income from that 
asset should be reported pursuant to Section 87207(b) (3). 
If the trust owns assets of both types, the appropriate 
provision should be applied to each asset. 
The staff also decided that Section 87206(e) applied to the 
disclosure of trust investments as well as investments held 
by business entities. 
Thus, if a person has a 50% interest 
in a trust, he would reveal all of the investments of the 
trust to the extent that they equaled $1,000 or more rather 
than merely disclosing those investments in which his pro 
rata share const 
d $1,000 or more. 
TP: jo

sections.conclusion: Section 87207(b) is the applicable provision for reporting trust income in the same way as income from business entity clients or assets. Section 87206(e) applies to trust investment disclosures, requiring disclosure of all trust investments equaling $1,000 or more based on total trust holdings, not pro rata share.
sections.question_synthetic: How should income from trust assets be reported for disclosure purposes, and what investment disclosure requirements apply to trust ownership interests?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Trust income should be reported under Section 87207(b) depending on the type of assets held (brokerage services vs. other services), and all trust investments of $1,000 or more must be disclosed regardless of the person's proportional interest in the trust.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document appears to be staff meeting notes rather than formal advice letter; lacks traditional Q/C section headers but contains clear staff determinations on two related disclosure issues. Memo format to meeting participants. Minor text truncation at end ('const d' appears incomplete).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: How should income from trust assets be reported for disclosure purposes, and what investment disclosure requirements apply to trust ownership interests?

CONCLUSION: Section 87207(b) is the applicable provision for reporting trust income in the same way as income from business entity clients or assets. Section 87206(e) applies to trust investment disclosures, requiring disclosure of all trust investments equaling $1,000 or more based on total trust holdings, not pro rata share.
embedding.first_500_words: Staie of California To From ora Opinion Request Meeting Participants FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Ted Prim Date 6-6-77 Subject; Disclosure of income of trusts. At the opinion request meeting of May 20, 1977, the staff determined that the income from the assets of a trust should be reported in the same way as the income from the clients or assets of a business entity. Thus, Section 87207(b) is the applicable provision. If the trust owns a business entity, it probably is a business trust and therefore a business entity pursuant to Section 82004. If an entity owned by a trust performs brokerage services, income from that asset should be reported pursuant to 87207(b) (2). If, however, the trust owns an asset which performs services other than legal or brokerage services, income from that asset should be reported pursuant to Section 87207(b) (3). If the trust owns assets of both types, the appropriate provision should be applied to each asset. The staff also decided that Section 87206(e) applied to the disclosure of trust investments as well as investments held by business entities. Thus, if a person has a 50% interest in a trust, he would reveal all of the investments of the trust to the extent that they equaled $1,000 or more rather than merely disclosing those investments in which his pro rata share const d $1,000 or more. TP: jo
embedding.summary: FPPC staff determined how trust income should be reported for disclosure purposes and clarified that trust investment disclosures must include all investments of $1,000 or more, not just those matching the filer's pro rata share.
